title: Polynomial Functions
blurb: Cubics and beyond: end behavior, zeros and multiplicity, the remainder and factor theorems, and sketching from factors.

lessons.title: Meet Polynomial Functions
lessons.concreteComparison: A rollercoaster with one dip is a parabola's world. Two dips and a hill? No quadratic can draw that — but $y = x^3 - 4x$ can. **Polynomial functions** are the rollercoaster family: every extra degree buys the curve one more possible turn, and this unit teaches you to read the whole track from the equation.
lessons.objective: Identify a polynomial function's degree and leading coefficient, evaluate it, and count its possible turning points.
concept: A **polynomial function** is $f(x) = a_nx^n + \cdots + a_1x + a_0$. The **degree** $n$ is the highest power; the **leading coefficient** $a_n$ rides the highest term. $f(x) = 2x^3 - 5x + 1$: degree 3, leading coefficient 2.
Degree names carry over: 1 linear, 2 quadratic, 3 **cubic**, 4 **quartic**, 5 **quintic**. Evaluating is Grade-10 substitution, just longer: $f(2) = 16 - 10 + 1 = 7$.
Degree buys turns: a degree-$n$ curve has at most $n - 1$ **turning points**. Lines never turn, parabolas turn once, cubics up to twice — that's the two-dips-and-a-hill budget.
Polynomial graphs are smooth and unbroken — no jumps (like rational functions at excluded values), no corners (like $|x|$). Any polynomial, any input: one clean curve.
lessons.keyIdea: Degree = highest power; it caps the turning points at n − 1. Smooth, unbroken curves — no jumps, no corners.

facts.title: Turn budget
facts.explanation: Each degree buys one more possible turn.

workedExamples.statement: How many turning points can a quartic (degree 4) have at most? A cubic?
workedExamples.solution: Quartic: up to 3; cubic: up to 2. (Budget: $n-1$.)

commonMistakes.text: Reading the degree off the FIRST term as written.
commonMistakes.correction: $f(x) = 5x - 2x^4 + 1$ has degree 4 — highest power anywhere. Reorder to standard form first, same as Grade 10.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking degree n means EXACTLY n − 1 turns.
commonMistakes.correction: $n-1$ is the BUDGET, not the bill: $y = x^3$ has degree 3 and zero turns. The cap is at most, never exactly.

tryIt.statement: Degree and leading coefficient of $f(x) = 7 - x^5 + 3x^2$?

steps.title: The rollercoaster family
beats: Lines: no turns. Parabolas: one turn. Then what?
$y = x^3 - 4x$: down, up, down — TWO turns.
Every extra degree buys one more possible turn.
Polynomials: the family that draws rollercoasters.

steps.title: Degree and leading coefficient
beats: Degree: highest power → 3 (a **cubic**).
Leading coefficient: rides the top term → 2.
These two numbers will run this whole unit.

steps.title: Read the vitals
steps.prompt: For $f(x) = 4x - x^6 + 2$, the degree and leading coefficient are…
steps.explanation: Highest power anywhere: $-x^6$ → degree 6, leading coefficient $-1$.

steps.title: Count the possible turns
steps.prompt: A degree-5 polynomial can have at most how many turning points?
steps.explanation: Budget is $n - 1 = 4$. (It may spend fewer — $x^5$ turns zero times.)

steps.title: Vitals and values
steps.intro: Standard form first; substitute with care.

examples.prompt: A curve rises, falls, rises, falls. What's the SMALLEST degree it could have?

steps.title: Family ID
steps.intro: Degree caps turns; the y-intercept is f(0).

problems.explanation: $16 - 20 + 4 = 0$ — so $x=2$ is a zero of this quartic.

problems.prompt: Which could NOT be the graph of a polynomial?
options: a curve with a jump (break) in it
problems.explanation: Polynomials are smooth and unbroken — jumps belong to rational functions at excluded values.
$f(0) = 9$ — the constant term, exactly like $c$ in Grade 10.

steps.title: Why engineers love polynomials
steps.body: Your phone renders curved fonts, your car's suspension is tuned, and Netflix compresses video — all with polynomials, because they're the only curves computers evaluate with just + and ×. Every other function (sines, logs, roots) gets APPROXIMATED by a polynomial before silicon touches it. This unit's family is computing's native tongue.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Degree = highest power; leading coefficient rides it.
Turn budget: at most $n - 1$ turning points.
Smooth, unbroken curves; $f(0)$ = the constant = y-intercept.

lessons.title: End Behavior
lessons.concreteComparison: Zoom a graph out far enough and every polynomial simplifies to its loudest voice: the leading term. $y = x^3 - 100x^2 + 5$ looks wild up close, but from a distance it's just $y = x^3$ — down-left, up-right. **End behavior** is reading the arms from two facts: is the degree even or odd, and is the leading coefficient positive or negative?
lessons.objective: Predict a polynomial's end behavior (both arms) from the degree's parity and the leading coefficient's sign.
concept: Far from the origin the leading term drowns out everything: at $x = 100$, $x^3$ is a million times $x$. So the ARMS of any polynomial match the arms of $\pm x^n$.
**Even degree**: both arms point the SAME way. Positive leading: both up ($\uparrow\ \uparrow$, like $x^2$). Negative: both down.
**Odd degree**: the arms point OPPOSITE ways. Positive leading: down-left, up-right ($\downarrow\ \uparrow$, like $x^3$). Negative: up-left, down-right.
Two questions decide all four cases: even or odd degree? positive or negative leading coefficient? $y = -2x^5 + 40x$: odd + negative → up-left, down-right — no other information needed.
lessons.keyIdea: Zoomed out, only the leading term speaks: even degree → arms together; odd → arms opposite; negative flips both.

facts.title: Why it works
facts.latex: x^3 \gg x^2 \gg x \text{ for large } x
facts.explanation: The leading term outshouts the rest.

workedExamples.solution: Odd degree, positive leading: down-left, up-right. (At $x = 200$: $x^3$ term is $8{,}000{,}000$ vs $-4{,}000{,}000$ — the cube already wins.)
Even degree, negative leading: BOTH arms down. Check: $f(10) = -30000 + 1000 - 7 < 0$ and $f(-10) < 0$.

workedExamples.statement: A graph's arms both point UP. What do you know about its equation?
workedExamples.solution: Even degree, positive leading coefficient — like $x^2$ or $3x^6$. Check $x^2$: $f(\pm 5) = 25 > 0$ both sides.

commonMistakes.text: Letting a big middle term change the verdict.
commonMistakes.correction: $y = x^3 - 100x^2$: the $-100x^2$ bends the middle, but at large $|x|$ the cube rules. Arms come from the LEADING term only.
Positive odd (like $x^3$) runs down-left, UP-right — it climbs as you read rightward. Up-left belongs to NEGATIVE odd. Anchor to $y = x^3$ and $y = x^2$; derive the rest by flipping.

steps.title: Zoom out
beats: $y = x^3 - 100x^2 + 5$: chaos up close.
At $x = 1000$: the cube is a BILLION; the rest, pocket change.
Zoomed out, every polynomial IS its leading term.
So the arms obey $\pm x^n$. Two facts decide everything.

steps.title: Degree and sign
steps.teach: Step the degree and flip the sign — the blue arrows call the arms. Even degrees point both arms the SAME way (together up, or together down); odd degrees split them. The negative flip turns the whole picture upside down. Four cases, two questions.

steps.title: Call the arms
steps.explanation: Even degree (4), negative leading: both down. The $99x^3$ shouts loudly in the middle, but the quartic owns the ends.

steps.title: Never memorize four cases
beats: Anchor 1: $y = x^2$ — even, both arms UP.
Anchor 2: $y = x^3$ — odd, down-left/up-right.
Negative? Flip the anchor upside down.
Two anchors + one flip = all four cases.

steps.title: Reverse reading
steps.prompt: A curve's arms run up-left and down-right. Its leading term could be…
steps.explanation: Opposite arms → odd degree; up-LEFT → negative leading. $-2x^5$ ✓.

examples.prompt: A polynomial's arms BOTH point down and it has 3 turning points. Smallest possible degree?
steps: Both down → even degree, negative leading.

problems.explanation: Even (2), negative: an upside-down parabola — both arms down.
Odd + positive: the right arm climbs. (At $x = 100$: $10^{10} - 9 \times 10^9 > 0$ — the five wins already.)

problems.prompt: Which equation could draw a curve whose arms BOTH rise?
problems.explanation: Both up needs even + positive: $x^4$ ✓. ($x^3$ splits; $-x^6$ points both down.)

steps.title: The million-dollar zoom-out
steps.body: Economists, epidemiologists, and climate modelers all exploit this lesson: whatever messy polynomial fits their data, LONG-RUN forecasts depend only on the leading term. Get the degree or its sign wrong and your 30-year projection points the wrong direction entirely — which is why 'what's the leading behavior?' is the first question in every modeling review.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Zoomed out, only the leading term matters.
Even degree: arms together. Odd: arms opposite.
Negative leading coefficient flips the whole picture.

lessons.title: Zeros & Factors
lessons.concreteComparison: Grade 10's zero-product property scaled up: $y = (x+2)(x-1)(x-3)$ crosses the x-axis exactly where a factor dies — at $-2$, $1$, and $3$. Factored form is a polynomial's landing log: every crossing listed by name. And it runs backwards: know the crossings, write the factors, rebuild the curve.
lessons.objective: Read zeros from factored form, build factored equations from zeros, and use the degree-n-has-at-most-n-zeros rule.
concept: A **zero** of $f$ is an input with $f(x) = 0$ — graphically, an x-intercept. For factored polynomials the zero-product property lists them: $(x+2)(x-1)(x-3) = 0$ at $x = -2, 1, 3$.
Backwards: zeros $-2, 1, 3$ → factors $(x+2)(x-1)(x-3)$ — each zero $r$ contributes the factor $(x - r)$, sneaky sign included.
Degree caps the count: a degree-$n$ polynomial has **at most $n$ zeros** (each crossing spends one factor). A cubic crosses at most 3 times.
The leading coefficient survives factoring: $y = 2(x+2)(x-1)(x-3)$ has the SAME zeros but doubles every height — same landings, steeper flight. Fix it with one extra point, usually the y-intercept.
lessons.keyIdea: Zeros = x-intercepts = factors' deaths. Zero r ↔ factor (x − r); degree n allows at most n of them.

workedExamples.solution: $x = -2, 1, 3$ — each factor's death. Check all three by substitution.

workedExamples.statement: Write a cubic with zeros $-1, 2, 4$ passing through standard height (leading 1), and verify one zero.

commonMistakes.text: Reading the zeros of $(x+2)(x-3)$ as $2$ and $-3$.
commonMistakes.correction: Each factor's ZERO: $x+2$ dies at $-2$; $x-3$ at $3$. The sneaky sign from vertex form and Grade 10 — it never went away.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting the leading coefficient when building from zeros.
commonMistakes.correction: Zeros $1, 2$ fit $y = a(x-1)(x-2)$ for ANY $a$ — infinitely many curves share those landings. A required extra point (often the y-intercept) pins $a$ down.

tryIt.statement: Build a quadratic with zeros $3$ and $-6$ (leading 1), and expand it.

steps.title: Factored form lists the crossings
beats: $y = 0$ exactly when a factor dies:
$x = -2$, $x = 1$, $x = 3$. Three landings, listed.
Zero-product property — now with three factors.

steps.title: Zeros drive the curve
steps.teach: The cubic's three crossings wear beacons. Step the THIRD zero and watch: the factor $(x - c)$ rewrites, the crossing slides, and the whole curve reshapes to land there. Factored form isn't just notation — it's the curve's flight plan.

steps.title: Read the log
steps.explanation: Each factor's death: $x = 4$, $x = -1$, $x = 2$. Signs flip on exit.

steps.title: From landings to equation
beats: Told: a cubic lands at $-1$, $2$, $4$.
Each zero $r$ → factor $(x - r)$:
The $a$? One more point pins it — often $f(0)$.

steps.title: Use the y-intercept
steps.prompt: A quadratic has zeros $1$ and $3$, and y-intercept $(0, 6)$. Then $a$ in $y = a(x-1)(x-3)$ is…
steps.explanation: $f(0) = a(-1)(-3) = 3a = 6 \to a = 2$. Same landings, height calibrated.

steps.title: Two-way dictionary
steps.intro: Factors → zeros; zeros → factors; a from a point.

examples.prompt: Write the cubic with zeros $-3, 0, 2$ and leading coefficient $1$, expanded.

steps.title: Landing-log drills
steps.intro: Sign flips on exit; count against the degree.

problems.prompt: How many zeros can $y = x^4 - 10x^2 + 9$ have at MOST?
problems.explanation: Degree 4 caps it at 4 — and this one hits the cap: $(x^2-1)(x^2-9)$ → $\pm1, \pm3$.

problems.prompt: A cubic crosses at $-2$ and $5$ only, passing straight through both. Could its third factor be $(x-5)$ again?
problems.explanation: $(x+2)(x-5)^2$ is a legal cubic — repeated factors are next lesson's star.
The 3 out front never zeroes — it just steepens. Landings: $1, -7$.

steps.title: The theorem behind the cap
steps.body: Why at most n zeros? The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra — proved by Gauss at age 21 — says a degree-n polynomial has EXACTLY n roots if you count repeats and allow complex numbers. Your 'at most n' is its real-axis shadow; the missing roots are hiding off the number line, waiting in this course's Complex Numbers topic.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Zero $r$ ↔ factor $(x-r)$: read and write both directions.
The leading $a$ needs one extra point — usually $f(0)$.

lessons.title: Multiplicity
lessons.concreteComparison: Three planes approach a runway. One lands and rolls straight through. One touches a wheel and lifts off again. One flattens out long and low before finally passing through. Same runway — three landing styles. Repeated factors give polynomial crossings the same three styles: **cross**, **bounce**, or **flatten-through** — and the exponent on the factor is the pilot.
lessons.objective: Use a zero's multiplicity to predict crossing style: odd multiplicity crosses (≥3 flattens), even multiplicity bounces.
concept: **Multiplicity** = the exponent on a zero's factor. In $y = (x+2)(x-1)^2$: zero $-2$ has multiplicity 1; zero $1$ has multiplicity 2.
**Odd multiplicity** (1, 3, 5…): the curve CROSSES the axis. Multiplicity 1 passes cleanly; 3 and up flatten out first, then pass — a slow-motion crossing.
**Even multiplicity** (2, 4…): the curve BOUNCES — touches the axis and turns back, never crossing. Near $x=1$, $(x-1)^2$ behaves like a little upward parabola glued to the axis.
Multiplicities spend the degree: $(x+2)(x-1)^2$ is degree $1 + 2 = 3$. The exponents must total the degree — a bookkeeping check that catches most sketch errors.
lessons.keyIdea: Multiplicity = factor's exponent. Odd → cross (≥3 flattens first); even → bounce. Multiplicities sum to the degree.

workedExamples.statement: For $y = (x+2)(x-1)^2$: list zeros with multiplicities and landing styles.
workedExamples.solution: $-2$ (mult 1): crosses. $1$ (mult 2): bounces. Check the bounce: $f(0.5) = 0.625 > 0$ and $f(1.5) = 0.875 > 0$ — same side!

workedExamples.statement: Does $y = (x-2)^2$ cross or bounce at $x = 2$? Verify with points either side.
workedExamples.solution: Bounces: $f(1) = 1 > 0$ and $f(3) = 1 > 0$ — the curve stays above, touching only at 2.

commonMistakes.text: Treating every zero as a clean crossing.
commonMistakes.correction: $(x-1)^2$ NEVER crosses at 1 — it bounces. Check a point either side: same sign = bounce, opposite = cross.

commonMistakes.text: Miscounting the degree by forgetting exponents.
commonMistakes.correction: $(x+2)(x-1)^2$ is degree 3, not 2 — the squared factor spends TWO degree points. Multiplicities must sum to the degree.

tryIt.statement: Verify the bounce of $y = (x+1)^2$ at $x = -1$ with points either side.

steps.title: Cross, bounce, flatten
beats: Multiplicity 1: land and roll through. CROSS.
Multiplicity 2: touch-and-go. BOUNCE.
Multiplicity 3: long, flat landing, then through.
The factor's exponent is the pilot.

steps.title: Step the exponent
steps.teach: The zero at x = 1 changes style as m steps: at 1 the curve passes cleanly; at 2 it kisses the axis and turns back (watch the beacon turn red); at 3 it squashes flat and slides through. One exponent, three different landings.

steps.title: Call the landing
steps.explanation: Multiplicity 2 = even = bounce. Verify: $f(4) = 5 > 0$, $f(6) = 7 > 0$ — same side.

steps.title: Verify any landing
beats: Unsure? Sample both sides of the zero.
Same sign both sides → bounce (even mult).
Opposite signs → cross (odd mult).
Two substitutions beat any memorized rule.

steps.title: Spend the degree
steps.explanation: $2 + 3 = 5$: multiplicities sum to the degree. (Bounce at $-1$, flatten-through at $2$.)

examples.prompt: Fully describe the zeros of $y = x^2(x-3)$.
steps: Degree $2+1 = 3$; arms of a positive cubic.

examples.prompt: Build a quartic (leading 1) that bounces at $x = 2$ and crosses at $x = -1$ and $x = 5$.

steps.title: Pilot calls
steps.intro: Even bounces; odd crosses; big odd flattens.

problems.explanation: Multiplicity 3: odd → through, but ≥3 → flattened first. ($f(-5) = -1$, $f(-3) = 1$: opposite sides ✓.)

problems.prompt: A quartic touches the axis at $1$ (turning back) and crosses at $-2$ and $3$. Its factorization shape is…
problems.explanation: Bounce = squared factor; two clean crossings; degree $2+1+1 = 4$ ✓. (The third option is degree 5.)
$1$ and $1$: same side of the axis on both flanks of the zero. That's what bouncing looks like in numbers.

steps.title: Bounces are how engineers spot double roots
steps.body: Control engineers tuning a drone WANT double roots — a response curve that touches its target and settles, instead of crossing into overshoot. When the simulation bounces off the setpoint line, they read it instantly: even multiplicity, critically damped, ship it. Your landing-style rule is their stability diagnostic.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Odd → cross (≥3 flattens first); even → bounce.
Multiplicities sum to the degree; the same-side test verifies.

lessons.title: The Remainder & Factor Theorems
lessons.concreteComparison: Is 3 a zero of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x^2 - 5x + 6$? The long way: divide by $(x-3)$ and check the remainder. The shortcut: just compute $f(3)$ — the **Remainder Theorem** says $f(3)$ IS that remainder. One substitution replaces an entire long division, and 'remainder zero' becomes the fastest factor test in algebra.
lessons.objective: Use the Remainder Theorem ($f(a)$ = remainder on division by $x-a$) and Factor Theorem ($f(a)=0 \iff (x-a)$ is a factor) to test and find factors.
concept: **Remainder Theorem**: dividing $f(x)$ by $(x - a)$ leaves remainder $f(a)$. Why: $f(x) = (x-a)q(x) + r$; plug in $x = a$ and the first term dies, leaving $f(a) = r$.
**Factor Theorem** (the payoff): $(x - a)$ is a factor exactly when $f(a) = 0$. Zero remainder = clean division = factor. It's the zero-factor dictionary with a proof attached.
Factor-hunting: test small candidates $\pm1, \pm2, \pm3, \ldots$ (divisors of the constant term). $f(x) = x^3 - 2x^2 - 5x + 6$: $f(1) = 0$ ✓ — so $(x-1)$ divides it.
Then peel: $f(x) = (x-1)(x^2 - x - 6) = (x-1)(x-3)(x+2)$ — one found zero reduces the cubic to a Grade-10 quadratic. Repeat until fully factored.
lessons.keyIdea: f(a) = the remainder on dividing by (x−a); f(a) = 0 means (x−a) is a factor. Test candidates, peel, repeat.

facts.title: Factor Theorem
facts.explanation: The fastest factor test in algebra.

workedExamples.statement: Find the remainder when $f(x) = x^3 - 2x^2 - 5x + 6$ is divided by $(x - 2)$.
Is $(x - 1)$ a factor of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x^2 - 5x + 6$? Fully factor if so.
For what $k$ is $(x + 1)$ a factor of $f(x) = x^3 + kx - 4$?

commonMistakes.text: Evaluating $f(-a)$ to test the factor $(x - a)$.
commonMistakes.correction: $(x - 3)$ dies at $x = +3$: test $f(3)$. For $(x + 3)$, test $f(-3)$. The factor's ZERO is what you substitute — sneaky sign, final boss form.

commonMistakes.text: Stopping after one factor is found.
commonMistakes.correction: $f(1) = 0$ gives $(x-1)$ — but the quotient $x^2 - x - 6$ still factors! Peel all the way down to linear pieces.

tryIt.statement: Remainder when $f(x) = x^3 + 2x - 7$ is divided by $(x-2)$?

steps.title: One substitution beats a division
beats: Divide $f$ by $(x-a)$: $f(x) = (x-a)q(x) + r$.
Now feed in $x = a$: the $(a-a)$ kills the big term.
$f(a) = r$. The value IS the remainder.
Whole long division, one substitution.

steps.title: Instant remainder
steps.prompt: The remainder when $x^3 - 4x + 1$ is divided by $(x-3)$ is…

steps.title: Zero remainder = factor
beats: If $f(a) = 0$, the division is CLEAN: $(x-a)$ divides $f$.
**Factor Theorem**: $f(a) = 0 \iff (x-a)$ is a factor.
Factor-testing is now just… evaluating.
The zero–factor dictionary, upgraded with a proof.

steps.title: Test small candidates, then peel
beats: $f(1) = 0$ ✓! So $(x-1)$ divides.
The quadratic is Grade 10: $(x-3)(x+2)$. Fully factored.

steps.title: Which value tests (x+3)?
steps.prompt: To test whether $(x + 3)$ is a factor of $f$, evaluate…
steps.explanation: $(x+3)$ dies at $x = -3$ — substitute the factor's ZERO. The sneaky sign's last stand.

steps.title: Hunt and peel
steps.intro: Evaluate to test; divide to peel; repeat.

examples.prompt: Find the remainder of $f(x) = 2x^3 - x^2 + 3$ divided by $(x + 1)$.
steps: Remainder 0 — a factor, discovered by accident!

problems.explanation: $f(1) = 1 - 1 = 0$ — a degree-100 division, solved by one substitution. THAT's the theorem's power.

problems.prompt: $f(x) = x^3 + x^2 - 10x + 8$: which is a factor?
$f(4) = 7$ tells you that dividing $f$ by $(x-4)$ leaves…
problems.explanation: The value at 4 IS the remainder; nonzero means the division isn't clean.

steps.title: The theorem that checks your ISBN
steps.body: Book codes, credit cards, and QR codes all verify themselves with remainder arithmetic — the checksum digit is chosen so a polynomial built from the digits leaves remainder zero. A scanner 'testing the factor' millions of times a day is running this lesson's theorem at the speed of light.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Hunt small candidates, peel to a quadratic, finish with Grade 10.

lessons.title: The Sketching Recipe
lessons.concreteComparison: You've collected the whole toolkit across five lessons: arms from the leading term, landings from the factors, landing STYLE from multiplicities, altitude from the y-intercept. Sketching a polynomial is now assembly, not artistry — a four-step recipe that turns $y = (x+2)(x-1)^2$ into a confident freehand curve in under a minute.
lessons.objective: Sketch factored polynomials with the four-step recipe: end behavior, zeros, multiplicities, y-intercept — then trace.
concept: The recipe: **(1)** arms from degree parity + leading sign; **(2)** zeros on the axis from the factors; **(3)** landing style at each zero from multiplicity; **(4)** y-intercept from $f(0)$. Then trace left-arm to right-arm through the landings.
Run it on $y = (x+2)(x-1)^2$: degree 3, positive → down-left/up-right; zeros $-2$ (cross), $1$ (bounce); $f(0) = 2$. Trace: rise from ↙, cross at $-2$, up through $(0,2)$, bounce at $1$, exit ↗.
The trace is forced: between consecutive zeros the curve can't touch the axis, so its sign is FIXED there — one sample point per gap confirms the arc.
Audit at the end: does the trace agree with both arms? Does each landing match its multiplicity? Does the curve pass the y-intercept? Three yeses = a trustworthy sketch.
lessons.keyIdea: Arms → zeros → landing styles → y-intercept, then trace. Between zeros the sign is fixed — sample once per gap.

facts.title: Gap rule
facts.explanation: One sample per gap pins the arc.

workedExamples.solution: Degree 3, +: ↙…↗. Cross at $-2$, bounce at $1$; $f(0) = 2$. Gap check: $f(-3) = -16 < 0$ ✓ left of $-2$ is below.
Degree 3, leading $-1$: ↖…↘. Crossings $0, 2, -3$; $f(0) = 0$ (origin!). Sample $f(1) = -1 \cdot -1 \cdot 4 = 4 > 0$: above between 0 and 2.

workedExamples.statement: Sketch data for $y = (x-2)^2(x+1)^2$: arms, landings, y-intercept.
workedExamples.solution: Degree 4, +: both arms UP. Bounces at $2$ AND $-1$ (never crosses!); $f(0) = 4$. The curve stays $\ge 0$ everywhere.

commonMistakes.text: Tracing landings right but arms wrong (or vice versa).
commonMistakes.correction: The trace must START and END on the leading term's arms. If your zigzag through the zeros exits the wrong corner, a multiplicity was misread — audit both.

commonMistakes.text: Letting the curve wander across the axis between zeros.
commonMistakes.correction: No zero in a gap = no crossing in that gap. The sign is locked; one sample point per gap confirms which side.

tryIt.statement: For $y = x(x-3)^2$: arms and landing styles?
tryIt.solution: Degree 3, +: ↙…↗; cross at 0, bounce at 3. ($f(1) = 4 > 0$ between them.)

tryIt.statement: What is $f(0)$ for $y = 2(x-1)(x+4)$, and which side of the axis is the curve there?
tryIt.solution: $f(0) = 2(-1)(4) = -8$: below the axis at the intercept.

steps.title: The four-step recipe
beats: 1. ARMS: degree parity + leading sign.
2. ZEROS: read the factors.
3. STYLES: multiplicity per zero.
4. $f(0)$: the altitude marker. Then trace.

steps.title: y = (x+2)(x−1)²
beats: Degree 3, positive: ↙ enters, ↗ exits.
Zeros: cross at $-2$, bounce at $1$.
Trace: rise, cross $-2$, over $(0,2)$, bounce at 1, exit ↗.

steps.title: The bounce in context
steps.teach: Here's that exact curve: cross at −2, up through the y-intercept, kiss-and-turn at 1 (the red beacon), exit up-right — every feature placed by the recipe. Step the multiplicity to 1 or 3 and re-run the recipe in your head: only step 3 changes, and the trace obeys.

steps.title: Recipe step 1
steps.explanation: Degree 3 (odd, arms opposite), leading $-2$ (negative, flip): up-left, down-right.

steps.title: Locked signs
steps.prompt: $y = (x+2)(x-1)^2$ has zeros only at $-2$ and $1$. Between them (say at $x = 0$) the curve is…
options: above the axis — and stays above on the whole gap
steps.explanation: $f(0) = 2 > 0$, and with no zero inside the gap it can't cross: the whole stretch rides above.

steps.title: Full recipes
steps.intro: All four steps, then the audit.

steps: Rise, cross $-2$, hump above, through 0, dip below, cross 2, exit ↗.
Rise to the bounce at $-1$, back down through $(0,-3)$, cross at 3, exit ↗.

steps.title: Recipe runs
steps.intro: Arms, zeros, styles, altitude — audit at the end.

problems.prompt: $y = (x-1)(x-4)^2$: at its y-intercept the curve is…
problems.explanation: $f(0) = (-1)(16) = -16$. The curve dips below before crossing at 1.

problems.prompt: A degree-4 curve with positive leading bounces at both of its only zeros. It ever goes below the axis…
problems.explanation: Both arms up + only bounces = the axis is touched, never crossed: the curve lives entirely at $y \ge 0$.

problems.prompt: Your trace of a cubic exits top-LEFT, but the leading term is $+x^3$. The audit says…
options: a landing style was misread — recheck multiplicities
problems.explanation: $+x^3$ MUST enter from ↙. A wrong exit means the crossings flipped the curve an extra time — some multiplicity is wrong.

steps.title: Freehand beats plotting
steps.body: Given ten minutes, a plotting robot computes 50 points; a mathematician runs this recipe in 30 seconds and draws a curve that's RIGHT everywhere it matters — arms, crossings, bounces, intercept. Exams, whiteboards, and engineering back-of-envelopes all reward structure over pixels. You now sketch like the mathematician.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Signs lock between zeros; one sample per gap.
Audit: trace must honor both arms and every landing style.

practice.statement: Degree, leading coefficient, and $f(1)$ for $f(x) = 3x^4 - x^2 + 5$?
Build a cubic (leading 1) with zeros $-1, 1, 6$; expand the first two factors.

practice.solution: Bounce at 4 (mult 2), cross at $-2$ (mult 1). Same-side check at 4: $f(3) = 5 > 0$, $f(5) = 7 > 0$ ✓.

practice.statement: Remainder when $f(x) = x^3 - 5x + 2$ is divided by $(x-2)$?

practice.solution: $f(-1) = 1 - 2 + 1 = 0$ ✓ yes. (It's $(x^2-1)^2$ — a double bounce... squared crossings!)

practice.statement: For $y = (x+3)(x-1)^2$: y-intercept, and the sign of the curve just left of $-3$?

testYourself.statement: Max turning points of a degree-6 polynomial, and $f(2)$ for $f(x) = x^6 - 60$?

testYourself.solution: Standard form leads with $-x^7$: odd, negative — up-left, down-right.

testYourself.statement: A quartic (leading 1) bounces at $0$ and crosses at $-2$ and $2$. Write it and give $f(1)$.
Sketch data for $y = -x(x-3)^2$: arms, landings, and the sign between $0$ and $3$.
testYourself.solution: Degree 3, leading $-1$: ↖…↘. Cross at 0, bounce at 3. Between: $f(1) = -4 < 0$ — below the axis.
